title: Rainstorm Refraction Studio
description: A broken glass window floats in the scene with a water bottle behind it. The user controls custom shader parameters to simulate rain streaks, refraction distortion, and chromatic aberration through the shattered glass, adjusting wetness, streak density, wind direction, frost opacity, tint color, and aberration intensity to craft a dramatic rainstorm view.
prompt:
Create a single HTML file using Three.js r170 that loads GlassBrokenWindow.glb and WaterBottle.glb via GLTFLoader from a CDN. Position the broken glass window at z=0 facing the camera and place the water bottle at z=-2 behind it, slightly off-center. Set up a PerspectiveCamera at z=3 with OrbitControls limited to a 45-degree arc. Add an HDR-style environment using a subtle ambient light (intensity 0.4) and a directional light (intensity 1.2) from upper-left to create dramatic shadows. Apply a custom ShaderMaterial to the glass window mesh that implements: (1) a rain streak effect using scrolling UV-based noise in the fragment shader, (2) screen-space refraction distortion that warps the view of the bottle behind it using a sampled render texture, and (3) chromatic aberration splitting RGB channels with configurable offset. Create a dat.GUI panel with these validated parameters: 'wetness' (float slider 0.0-1.0, default 0.3, controls specular glossiness of glass surface), 'streakDensity' (int slider 1-50, default 15, controls number of rain streaks via noise frequency), 'windAngle' (float slider 0-360, default 45, rotates the rain streak direction in the shader), 'frostOpacity' (float slider 0.0-1.0, default 0.1, blends a white frosted overlay onto the glass), 'tintColor' (color picker, default #aaccff, multiplied into the glass fragment output), and 'aberrationStrength' (float slider 0.0-0.05, default 0.01, controls RGB channel separation distance). Each parameter must be passed as a uniform to the ShaderMaterial and update in real-time. Render the scene behind the glass to a WebGLRenderTarget so the fragment shader can sample it for refraction. The vertex shader should pass UV coordinates and world-space normals. The fragment shader should combine the refracted background sample, rain streaks, frost overlay, tint, and chromatic aberration into the final color. Validate that all slider values clamp within their defined ranges. Ensure the animation loop updates a time uniform for animating rain streaks downward.

[State Exposure (required)]
Expose window.__3D_STATE__ updated every animation frame:
window.__3D_STATE__ = {
  wetness: number,         // specular glossiness of glass surface (0.0-1.0)
  streakDensity: number,         // number of rain streaks via noise frequency (1-50)
  windAngle: number,         // rotation angle of rain streak direction in degrees (0-360)
  frostOpacity: number,         // opacity of frost effect on glass (0.0-1.0)
  chromaticOffset: number,         // RGB channel separation distance for chromatic aberration (0.0-0.05)
  refractionStrength: number,         // intensity of refraction distortion (0.0-2.0)
  tintColorHex: string,         // hex color code for glass tint effect
  rainSpeed: number,         // animation speed of rain streaks (0.0-5.0)
  glassLoaded: boolean,         // whether GlassBrokenWindow.glb has loaded successfully
  bottleLoaded: boolean,         // whether WaterBottle.glb has loaded successfully
  shaderActive: boolean,         // whether custom shader material is applied to glass
  cameraDistance: number,         // distance of camera from origin (1.0-5.0)
}
